nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still brooks and small streams in lowland coastal rainforest; soft, tannin-stained water over leaf litter and submerged vegetation; calcium-containing soil substrates also documented

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, fine-leaved stem plants, floating cover)
bogwood and leaf litter for tannins and shelter
dim lighting or surface cover to reduce stress

diet.notes: Strongly prefers live and frozen invertebrate foods: mosquito larvae, daphnia, brine shrimp, cyclops, micro-worms, and white worms. Will often refuse or ignore dry foods initially; high-quality micro-pellets or flakes may be accepted after conditioning. Feed two to three small portions daily. Varied live/frozen diet has been associated with notably higher spawning rates.

goodWith: other small, non-boisterous West African species
avoidWith: fast or boisterous tankmates that outcompete at feeding

summary: A jewel-like non-annual West African killifish from Nigeria and Cameroon, the Two-Stripe Lyretail reaches ~5 cm and is celebrated for the male’s lyrate caudal fin, bold two-stripe pattern, and multiple brilliantly coloured locality forms (“Funge”, “Biafra”, “Lagos Red”). Intermediate care; requires a tight lid, soft acidic water, and a diet of live or frozen invertebrates.
careNotes: The Two-Stripe Lyretail is a non-annual killifish from the lowland coastal rainforest streams of southeastern Nigeria and southwestern Cameroon. Unlike annual killifish, it does not rely on a drought-driven diapause cycle and can be maintained continuously in the aquarium without dry-incubation of eggs. Males are strikingly coloured with a golden-brown dorsum, iridescent blue-green flanks, vivid red spotting on the fins, and the hallmark elongated, lyre-shaped caudal fin; females are plainer and smaller. The hobby recognises several locality forms that differ in fin and body colour pattern, most commonly “Funge” (Cameroon origin) and “Biafra” (Nigerian origin), as well as “Lagos Red” and “TAAG 2000” strains. A species-only or carefully chosen community setup is strongly recommended: a dedicated pair or trio (one male, two females) in a 40–60 L planted tank is the most reliable arrangement. Males show mild intraspecific territorial behaviour but rarely cause injury when adequate visual breaks (plants, bogwood) are present. The species is a confirmed jumper and a tight-fitting lid is essential. Water should be soft and slightly acidic (pH 5.8–7.0, gH 1–8); peat or blackwater extract filtration helps maintain the tannin-rich conditions the fish favours and enhances colour expression. Temperature is ideally 22–26°C; higher end (24–26°C) suits general maintenance and slightly lower (22–24°C) suits long-term breeding. Although FishBase notes difficulty in aquarium maintenance, experienced killifish keepers report reasonable hardiness once established in a stable, well-planted soft-water setup.
breedingNotes: A substrate or plant spawner that deposits eggs individually into Java moss, spawning mops, or fine-leaved plants rather than burying them in peat as annual killifish do. Eggs can be harvested from mops and transferred to a separate shallow tray or container with moist peat or a small volume of soft water at ~22°C; they hatch in approximately 10–21 days depending on temperature. Alternatively, eggs may be left in a densely planted breeding tank and fry collected as they appear. Fry are tiny; first foods should be infusoria or very fine nauplii, graduating to micro-worms and freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. A slightly elevated temperature (24–26°C) and a diet rich in live brine shrimp have been associated with significantly higher egg production. Breeding difficulty is moderate: conditioning adults on live foods and maintaining stable soft-water parameters are the primary requirements.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aphyosemion bivittatum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (southeastern Nigeria and southwestern Cameroon), adult size (to ~45 mm SL), temperature (24–30°C), pH (5.0–6.5), hardness (0–179 ppm), habitat (lowland coastal rainforest brooks), non-annual status, locality forms (Funge, Biafra, Lagos Red), and lyrate caudal-fin sexual dimorphism. Note: SeriouslyFish profile is listed under the synonym Aphyosemion bitaeniatum for the bitaeniatum complex but covers bivittatum locality data.

additional.name: FishBase — Aphyosemion bivittatum
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 5.0 cm TL), temperature (22–24°C), pH (6.0–6.5), native range (southeastern Nigeria and southwestern Cameroon), habitat (coastal rainforest brooks with calcium-containing soil), taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), non-annual status confirmed. FishBase notes the species “is difficult to maintain in aquarium.”

additional.name: Aquadiction — Two-Stripe Lyretail Killifish Care Guide
additional.notes: Provided gH range (1–6), temperature (22–25°C), pH (5.5–7.0), compatible species list, diet details (live/frozen insect larvae, brine shrimp, daphnia, mosquito larvae), escape risk (tight lid required), intermediate care level, breeding method (Java moss / spawning mops, 14–21 day incubation), lifespan (up to 3 years), and locality form notes (Funge strain featured).

additional.name: Tropical Freshwater Fish — Aphyosemion bivittatum
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (to 5 cm / 2 in), tank size (10 gal / 38 L for a pair), pH (5.8–7.0, ideal 6.5), hardness (1–8 dH, ideal 3), temperature (23–26°C), diet (insects, insect larvae, worms, crustaceans; may accept flakes), temperament (peaceful), and breeding (eggs hatch 10–12 days, fry fed rotifers and small nauplii).

additional.name: killi.co.uk — Aphyosemion bivittatum Species Profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist breeding reports confirming spawning-mop method (63% of respondents), long-term tank breeding (38%), temperature range 20–27°C, diet preferences (white worms, brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, mosquito larvae), locality strains (Biafra, Funge, TAAG 2000), and notable hardiness reports from experienced keepers.

fish.breedingNotes: Non-annual plant/mop spawner; eggs deposited individually in Java moss or spawning mops and hatch in 10–21 days at 22–24°C. Fry require infusoria or very fine nauplii as first food. Conditioning adults on live brine shrimp markedly increases egg production.
